title: 1 Corinthians 16
subtitle: Final Instructions and Greetings

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — "The Collection for the Lord's People"

heb.paragraph: The noun logeia (collection) is a rare term, attested in Egyptian papyri as a special levy. Paul avoids common terms for money-gathering in favour of this neutral term. The collection for Jerusalem serves both practical charity and theological symbolism: Gentile churches supporting Jewish believers demonstrates the unity of the one people of God (cf. Rom 15:25–27; 2 Cor 8–9; Gal 2:10).

The verb euodoō (to prosper) in v. 2 is used in the divine passive: 'in keeping with his income.' The proportional principle anticipates the later teaching of 2 Cor 8–9. Paul's instruction to set aside money weekly on 'the first day of every week' provides early evidence for Sunday as the regular day of Christian assembly.

refs.note: The theological rationale: 'If the Gentiles have shared in the Jews' spiritual blessings, they owe it to share material blessings.' The collection is reciprocal grace.
Paul's extended treatment provides the theological depth: giving as response to Christ's self-emptying grace (8:9), cheerful generosity (9:7), and the goal of equality (8:13–14).
At the Jerusalem Council, the 'pillars' asked Paul to 'remember the poor.' The collection fulfils this commitment.

notes.note: Paul establishes systematic, proportional, weekly giving. The 'first day of every week' confirms Sunday as the gathering day. The purpose is both practical and theological.
Paul's administrative care: letters of recommendation for delegates, possible personal accompaniment. This attention to transparency reflects his concern to be 'above reproach' (2 Cor 8:20–21).

Calvin draws the principle that Christian giving should be regular, proportional, and organised. The weekly discipline trains the believer in generosity. Paul's care for financial transparency is a model for church leaders. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note explains that the 'Galatian churches' received the same instructions, confirming a multi-church project.
The NET note on 'the first day of every week' observes this is the earliest explicit reference to Sunday as the regular day of assembly.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee emphasises the ecclesiological significance: the collection embodies the unity of God's people across ethnic and geographical boundaries. Paul's instructions are a model of stewardship. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton argues the collection functioned as an eschatological sign: the Gentile pilgrimage to Jerusalem with gifts anticipated the prophetic vision of nations streaming to Zion (Isa 2:2–4; 60:1–14). This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: The collection for the Jerusalem saints was a project of enormous theological significance, demonstrating the organic unity of the church. Giving is weekly (Sunday), proportional (according to income), and systematic. Paul's concern to avoid collections during his visit suggests he wants no appearance of financial pressure. The letters of introduction follow standard ancient epistolary practice.

heb.paragraph: The verb epimenō (to remain) in v. 8 indicates Paul's decision to stay in Ephesus until Pentecost despite opposition. The compound form (epi + menō) suggests deliberate resolve.

The noun thyra (door) in v. 9 is used metaphorically for a ministry opportunity. The juxtaposition of 'a great door' and 'many who oppose me' captures the Pauline paradox: effective ministry and fierce opposition coexist.

refs.note: Luke confirms Paul's plan to travel through Macedonia and Achaia.
Paul later explains a change of plan that became controversial.

notes.note: Paul's pastoral strategy: an extended visit, not a brief stopover. The 'open door' in Ephesus is sufficient reason to delay. Paul models balancing present opportunities and future planning.
Timothy carries apostolic authority and should be received without contempt. Apollos' independent decision not to visit suggests genuine freedom within the apostolic team.

Calvin commends Paul's prayerful planning: make wise plans but hold them loosely, always subject to divine redirection. The protection of Timothy reminds that younger ministers need senior advocacy. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Pentecost as a calendar marker confirms Paul's Jewish heritage and suggests 1 Corinthians was written in the spring. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

The NET note suggests Apollos may have declined to avoid exacerbating the factionalism associated with his name. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads the travel plans as evidence that Paul expected the letter to resolve major issues before his arrival. The Apollos detail subtly undermines the faction system: Paul and Apollos are colleagues, not rivals. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton reconstructs the chronology and notes that Timothy's relationship to the letter itself is uncertain. The instruction to treat Timothy without contempt reflects Graeco-Roman social dynamics where youth and humble origin invited dismissal. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Paul intends to travel through Macedonia before Corinth, where he hopes to spend the winter. He is in Ephesus, where ministry flourishes despite opposition. Timothy's anticipated visit and Apollos' reluctance reveal relational dynamics within the apostolic team. Paul's protective language about Timothy suggests youth and perhaps temperamental caution.

heb.paragraph: The Aramaic phrase marana tha (Our Lord, come!) in v. 22 is one of the earliest preserved liturgical formulae, retained in Aramaic even in a Greek letter. Its preservation indicates origin in the earliest Aramaic-speaking Palestinian church. The phrase also appears in Didache 10.6 and Rev 22:20. It expresses the eschatological longing for Christ's return.

The noun anathema in v. 22 translates the Hebrew herem—something irrevocably devoted to God for destruction (Josh 6:17). The juxtaposition of anathema and marana tha creates a stark liturgical sequence: curse on the unfaithful, prayer for the Lord's coming.

refs.note: Paul's autograph parallels 1 Cor 16:21, confirming his use of an amanuensis with a closing greeting in his own hand.
The closing grace benediction is a standard Pauline formula.
John's 'Come, Lord Jesus' translates the Aramaic marana tha into Greek.

notes.note: Five rapid imperatives: be watchful, stand firm, be courageous, be strong—and do everything in love. The first four are military; the fifth is the distinctively Christian qualifier.

The household of Stephanas: Paul's first converts in Achaia, devoted to service. With Fortunatus and Achaicus (who may have delivered the Corinthians' letter), they model organic, service-based leadership.

Greetings from the Asian churches. Aquila and Priscilla with their house church. The 'holy kiss,' the autograph, the anathema-marana tha formula, and the grace-love benediction bring the letter to a solemn and affectionate close. Paul's unique closing—'My love to all of you in Christ Jesus'—ends the letter as an act of the agapē it proclaims.

Calvin reads the five imperatives as a practical summary of the letter. The commendation of Stephanas illustrates that church leadership arises from faithful service. The marana tha formula shows the eschatological expectation was central to the earliest church.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note discusses the parsing: marana tha (imperative, 'Our Lord, come!') vs. maran atha (confessional, 'Our Lord has come'). The NET translators favour the imperative reading based on Rev 22:20.
The NET note explains the ancient practice of autograph authentication.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, 1 Corinthians, NICNT (1987) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues the closing models the community Paul has been urging: service-based leadership, trans-congregational unity, physical affection crossing social boundaries, and eschatological accountability. Paul's unique closing—'My love to all of you'—is itself an act of the agapē the letter proclaims.

thiselton.source: Anthony C. Thiselton, 1 Corinthians, NIGTC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Thiselton notes the military vocabulary of vv. 13–14 resonated with Corinthian familiarity with Roman legionary culture. The marana tha receives extensive treatment: its preservation in Aramaic indicates a fixed element of the eucharistic liturgy. The letter's closing is a microcosm of its message.

hist.context: The closing transitions from travel logistics to rapid-fire exhortations (vv. 13–14), commendations (vv. 15–18), greetings (vv. 19–20), and a final autograph and blessing (vv. 21–24). The five imperatives of v. 13 condense the letter's message. 'Do everything in love' (v. 14) reprises ch. 13. Paul's autograph (v. 21) authenticates the letter. The marana tha preserves the liturgical voice of the earliest Palestinian church.

themes.note: Mission dominates as the chapter focuses on practical coordination across churches. Faith and Justice score well given the emphasis on standing firm and supporting the Jerusalem poor.

rows.label: vv.1–4
rows.text: The collection for Jerusalem: weekly, proportional, organised

rows.label: vv.5–12
rows.text: Travel plans: Macedonia route, Ephesian ministry, Timothy and Apollos

rows.label: vv.13–14
rows.text: Five imperatives: watchfulness, firmness, courage, strength, love

rows.label: vv.19–24
rows.text: Greetings, holy kiss, autograph, anathema-marana tha, benediction

lit.note: Chapter 16 concludes with practical matters, leadership commendation, and a liturgical closing weaving eschatological urgency with pastoral affection.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Apollos
ppl.role: Eloquent Alexandrian teacher; discipled by Priscilla and Aquila
ppl.text: Apollos is an Alexandrian Jew who arrives at Ephesus knowing the way of God accurately but only knowing John’s baptism (Acts 18:24–25). After Priscilla and Aquila complete his instruction, he becomes a powerful apologist in Achaia, "vigorously refuting his Jewish opponents in public debate" (Acts 18:28). Paul’s Corinthian correspondence shows Apollos became an unintended source of factionalism (1 Cor 1:12), though Paul regards him as a fellow worker.

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Calvin draws the principle that Christian giving should be regular, proportional, and organised…

ppl.name: Stephanas
ppl.text: The household of Stephanas: Paul's first converts in Achaia, devoted to service…

ppl.name: Timothy
ppl.role: Paul’s young protégé; co-author of six NT letters
ppl.text: Timothy is recruited at Lystra on the second journey (Acts 16:1–3) — the son of a Jewish mother and Greek father, circumcised by Paul to aid Jewish outreach. He becomes Paul’s most trusted emissary, sent to troubled churches when Paul cannot go. Paul twice calls him "my true son in the faith" and addresses two personal letters to him. He is co-author of six Pauline letters and appears at the end of Hebrews.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Final Instructions and Greetings

translations.text: Then, when I arrive, I will give letters of introduction to the men you approve and send them with your gift to Jerusalem.
And when I arrive, I will send those whom you accredit by letter to carry your gift to Jerusalem.
On the first day of every week, each one of you should set aside a sum of money in keeping with your income, saving it up, so that when I come no collections will have to be made.
On the first day of every week, each of you is to put something aside and store it up, as he may prosper, so that there will be no collecting when I come.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Paul establishes systematic, proportional, weekly giving. The 'first day of every week' confirms Sunday as the gathering day. The purpose is both practical and theological."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin draws the principle that Christian giving should be regular, proportional, and organised. The weekly discipline trains the believer in generosity. Paul's care for financial transparency is a mo…"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The NET note explains that the 'Galatian churches' received the same instructions, confirming a multi-church project."

thread.text: The theological rationale: 'If the Gentiles have shared in the Jews' spiritual blessings, they owe it to share material blessings.' The collection is reciprocal grace.
Paul's extended treatment provides the theological depth: giving as response to Christ's self-emptying grace (8:9), cheerful generosity (9:7), and the goal of equality (8:13–14).
At the Jerusalem Council, the 'pillars' asked Paul to 'remember the poor.' The collection fulfils this commitment.
Luke confirms Paul's plan to travel through Macedonia and Achaia.
Paul later explains a change of plan that became controversial.
Paul's autograph parallels 1 Cor 16:21, confirming his use of an amanuensis with a closing greeting in his own hand.

discourse.thesis: The collection for Jerusalem, travel plans, and commendations of co-workers are the concrete expressions of the gospel's power to create one trans-ethnic, mutually caring community.

nodes.text: The collection for the saints: as I directed the churches of Galatia, so you also are to do. On the first day of every week, each of you is to put something aside and store it up, as he may prosper — so that there will be no collecting when I come. Those the Corinthians approve, Paul will send with letters to carry their gift to Jerusalem.

Paul's travel plans: he will visit them after passing through Macedonia. He wants to spend time with them — perhaps winter there so they can help him on his journey. He will stay in Ephesus until Pentecost because a wide door has opened to him (θύρα μεγάλη ... ἀνέῳγεν), though there are many adversaries. Timothy is coming — receive him without causing him fear. Apollos will come later when opportunity arises.

children.text: The open door metaphor: effective ministry creates opposition, not the absence of it. Paul stays in Ephesus precisely because of both the opportunity and the adversaries — both are signs of fruitful ground. The Corinthians would do well to understand their own conflicts as signs of serious engagement, not reasons to retreat.

nodes.text: Final exhortations: be watchful, stand firm in the faith, act like men, be strong. Let all that you do be done in love (ἐν ἀγάπῃ — the same love that has governed the whole letter's argument). Commendation of the house of Stephanas — the firstfruits of Achaia, who devoted themselves to service. Be subject to such people and to every fellow worker and laborer.

children.text: The single governing instruction — 'let all that you do be done in love' — is the letter's ethical summary. After 15 chapters of instruction, correction, and argument, the last word is love. The letter ends where ch. 13 is centered.

nodes.text: Greetings from the churches of Asia, Aquila and Prisca. Greet one another with a holy kiss. Paul writes the greeting in his own hand. The anathema on anyone who does not love the Lord (if anyone does not love the Lord, let him be accursed). Marana tha (Our Lord, come). The grace of the Lord Jesus be with you. My love be with you all in Christ Jesus.

children.text: Marana tha (Aramaic: 'Our Lord, come') is one of the earliest preserved Christian prayers — preserved in Aramaic in a Greek letter written to a Greek-speaking community. It places the Corinthian congregation within the stream of Jewish Christianity and the universal early church, praying together for the return of the same Lord.

discourse.note: 1 Corinthians 16 grounds the letter's theology in practical generosity, relational planning, and community commendation. The collection for Jerusalem (v. 1-4) is not administrative detail but the practical expression of the one-body argument: Gentile churches support Jewish believers, embodying the reconciliation Paul has argued throughout. The letter ends with love (v. 14, v. 24) — the word that encompasses the entire letter's purpose.

coaching.tip: 'On the first day of every week, each of you is to put something aside' (v. 2). Systematic, proportional giving for the Jerusalem collection. Paul doesn't guilt-trip but organizes. Generosity is planned, not impulsive. The Corinthians' abundance serves Jerusalem's poverty. The church's economy is redistributive.

'If anyone has no love for the Lord, let him be accursed. Our Lord, come!' (v. 22). The letter ends with warning and longing. 'Maranatha' — Aramaic for 'Our Lord, come!' — preserves the earliest church's prayer. After 16 chapters of correction, Paul's heart: love the Lord, long for his return.

prayer_prompt: As Paul closes with 'Be on your guard; stand firm in the faith; be courageous; be strong — and do everything in love,' consider asking God which of those five commands you most need to hear today.
